text: This study investigates coaching and equipping children for service as a paradigm for following the example of Christ. The hypothesis for this study is that the youth is part of the church. The church with children as part of it, received gifts from God and these gifts are utilised for the building up of the church. In terms of the building up of the church, children together with parents and parents bear a huge responsibility of coaching and convoying children to be obedient to their calling as followers of Jesus Christ. In terms of the diakonia of children, the word diakonia is utilised in order to indicate that parents and children together with the church must utilise their gifts for their own diakonia. In recent research diakonia has been given a broader context. This study explores the research field in four phases and therefore attempts to get to the bottom of the practical-theological situation. Phase one attempted a descriptive reflection of research in practical theology. Phase two included an exploratory research from the field of social psychology that showed the incorrect attitudes and lack of convoying of children in the praxis of the church and thus had an intra-disciplinary conversation with the child development psychology. Phase three brought basic-theoretical insights from Scripture in order to show how the praxis of the church should be, and phase four presented practical-theoretical insights in order to confirm the responsibility of the church for coaching and equipping children in their diakonia. The conclusion drawn from the research is that children who are involved in diakonia and therefore who are obedient to Christ, can also be people who show the Jesus Christ attitude towards the world. Therefore parents and other believers must coach children in their diakonia, in order to also bring about spiritual maturity in the lives of the children.
headline: Fully Human Written
